coredata.dc:description:
Background The current study demonstrates a process to produce a hybrid bio-organic cellulose nanofiber (CNF) membrane with high filtration performance and mechanical strength. This was accomplished by 1) tailoring the functional groups of CNFs, notably using the 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine-1-oxyl (TEMPO) radical oxidation and 2) coupling them with carbon dots (CDs).
Methods The surface chemistry of CNF was modified through TEMPO oxidation. Subsequently, a coupling reaction was conducted with CDs synthesized hydrothermally, as well as synthesized N/S-CDs derived from palm bunch. The impact of these modifications on the formation of air filters was investigated. Modification and coupling of surface modified CNF were evaluated by advanced analytical techniques. The toxicity of pristine and N/S-CDs coupled cellulose nanofibers was studied by cytotoxicity assay. Vacuum filtration and sequential compression molding techniques were applied to fabricate CNF filters, and the particle filtration efficiency of different filters was determined.
Significant findings The coupling of N/S-CDs to CNF significantly enhanced the tensile strength of the membranes. Coupling of N/S-CDs to TEMPOCNF membrane showed the highest tensile strength (9.3 ± 1.9 MPa), while N/S-CDs coupling to CNF membrane exhibited slightly lower tensile strength (9.0 ± 1.3 MPa). The average pore diameter of the filters was slightly reduced after the CNF surface modification, which led to higher differential pressure across the filters. Both the pristine and TEMPO-modified CNF filters displayed high filtration efficiency for 0.3 μm-aerosol particles (∼70–81 %) and 0.1 μm-aerosol model particles (∼50–68 %), which was in a range of bacteria and viruses. This study provided detailed information about the fabrication of modified CNF filters with high air filtration efficiency for micro/nano-sized particles using cost-effective biodegradable raw materials.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The current study demonstrates a process to produce a hybrid bio-organic cellulose nanofiber (CNF) membrane with high filtration performance and mechanical strength. This was accomplished by 1) tailoring the functional groups of CNFs, notably using the 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine-1-oxyl (TEMPO) radical oxidation and 2) coupling them with carbon dots (CDs).

The surface chemistry of CNF was modified through TEMPO oxidation. Subsequently, a coupling reaction was conducted with CDs synthesized hydrothermally, as well as synthesized N/S-CDs derived from palm bunch. The impact of these modifications on the formation of air filters was investigated. Modification and coupling of surface modified CNF were evaluated by advanced analytical techniques. The toxicity of pristine and N/S-CDs coupled cellulose nanofibers was studied by cytotoxicity assay. Vacuum filtration and sequential compression molding techniques were applied to fabricate CNF filters, and the particle filtration efficiency of different filters was determined.

The coupling of N/S-CDs to CNF significantly enhanced the tensile strength of the membranes. Coupling of N/S-CDs to TEMPOCNF membrane showed the highest tensile strength (9.3 ± 1.9 MPa), while N/S-CDs coupling to CNF membrane exhibited slightly lower tensile strength (9.0 ± 1.3 MPa). The average pore diameter of the filters was slightly reduced after the CNF surface modification, which led to higher differential pressure across the filters. Both the pristine and TEMPO-modified CNF filters displayed high filtration efficiency for 0.3 μm-aerosol particles (∼70–81 %) and 0.1 μm-aerosol model particles (∼50–68 %), which was in a range of bacteria and viruses. This study provided detailed information about the fabrication of modified CNF filters with high air filtration efficiency for micro/nano-sized particles using cost-effective biodegradable raw materials.

ce:section.ce:para: Air pollution has become a major global concern in recent times. Conventional air pollutants, such as nitrogen oxides (NOx) and particulate matter (PM), continue to raise major concerns about environmental degradation [1]. The presence of chemicals, PM, and biological contaminants in the air can cause several diseases and discomfort in both human beings and other living organisms. The air becomes toxic and harmful due to the intricate combination of PM and toxic substances [2,3]. PM consists of a diverse range of particles of different sizes and liquid droplets [4]. Particles with dimensions less than 2.5 and 10 μm are commonly referred to as PM2.5 and PM10, respectively [5]. In the past few years, developing nations have been grappling with significant concerns for air pollution, especially PM contamination. A large portion of the population is exposed to hazardous and low-quality air. High air pollution is the main cause of early fatalities. According to the literature, in 2012, air pollution caused over seven million deaths worldwide [4]. This means that the number of people dying from a disease caused by air pollution is more than twice the previous estimates. PM2.5 particles are one of the most hazardous pollutants, entering the bronchi and lungs, resulting in artery plaque, which can ultimately cause vascular inflammation, coronary artery disease, stroke and other cardiovascular disorders. Consequently, prolonged exposure to PM 2.5 can lead to a range of health issues [6–8]. Nowadays, the condition of indoor air has become a significant concern as people spend more time inside buildings. To enhance the quality of interior air, buildings are now equipped with air filtration or purification systems. Air purification methods like mechanical filtration, photocatalytic oxidation, activated carbon adsorption, plasma air purification, and heat-based purification have been used for many years; nevertheless, maintaining continuous exchange of air requires a large amount of energy and high cost. Consequently, there is a need for multi-functional filters that offer both high filtration efficiency and low air flow resistance. The choice of methods depends on individual needs, concerns about indoor air quality, types of contaminants, and any pre-existing health issues [9,10]. While these air filtering systems effectively remove numerous contaminants and enhance indoor air quality, they tend to be energy-intensive and expensive. One solution of air purification technology that offers selective filtering, such as a high-efficiency particulate air (HEPA) filter, can remove particles smaller than 0.3 µm.

Filtration with nanofibrous filters facilitates multiple size-dependent particle capture techniques that work together to capture micro-sized particulates. These methods include interception, diffusion, inertial impaction, and electrostatic attraction. Although traditional air filters can filter PM2.5-PM10, they are unable to chemically capture and filter hazardous gaseous molecules [11]. In addition, it is essential to consider that the materials used in the fabrication of filters should not emit hazardous compounds or particulates during use, which is known as secondary pollution [12]. Synthetic polymers, glass, and petroleum materials are commonly used in the fabrication of filters. However, there is a promising alternative material for air filtration membranes called cellulose. Cellulose is biocompatible and environmentally friendly, with excellent strength and mechanical stability [13]. The nanoscale size of cellulose nanofibers (CNF) exhibits a large surface area, making them highly effective in adsorption and filtration. Additionally, cellulose is biocompatible, ensuring that the filter is in direct contact with humans or sensitive materials [14,15]. Furthermore, cellulose can be easily customized in terms of pore size, thickness, and surface functionalization. The most commonly used method for functionalization of cellulose is TEMPO oxidization [16]. The presence of carboxylate groups in the TEMPO oxidized CNF enhances its chemical reactivity, making it highly reactive. Additionally, the negatively charged carboxylate groups in TEMPO functionalized CNF enable it to capture contaminants of different sizes effectively. However, introducing nanoparticles as a spacer to modify the porosity of a non-compacted CNF filter can lead to a reduction in the porosity of a CNF filter due to limited interactions between the nanoparticles and CNF [17]. Carbon dot (CD) is a highly attractive carbon nanomaterial that exhibits a strong affinity for various polymers, including CNF [18]. Despite this, there have been only a limited number of studies on the interaction of CDs with CNF matrices using physical or chemical techniques.

The present work focuses on the functionalization of CNF for air filtration purposes. To achieve this, we conducted TEMPO oxidation of CNF followed by coupling with N/S-CDs. The coupling process between N/S-CDs and both native and TEMPO-oxidized cellulose was carried out at 30 °C and in hydrothermal reactors. The successful coupling and surface modification of CNF was confirmed using advanced techniques. For the fabrication of the affinity membranes, we opted for a scalable process, namely vacuum filtration. The fabricated filters displayed excellent mechanical strength and were non-toxic, eco-friendly, and biocompatible. The effectiveness of these filters in removing model particles representing viruses and bacteria aerosols of size 0.1 μm and 0.3 μm was reported. This study introduced a novel concept of fully bio-based, sustainable hybrid affinity membranes that possess a remarkable capacity for air filtration. Our selective regional functionalization approach can be applied to any bio-based material, enabling the production of efficient functionalized affinity membranes for air contaminants removal.

Empty fruit bunches (EFB) is one of the oilseed crop residues found abundantly in Southeast Asia [19]. Cellulose nanofibers (CNF) derived from oil palm EFB (purity: 99.9 %) were purchased from Zoep Nano Sdn. Bhd., Malaysia. Formic acid (98/100 %; analytical grade) was purchased from Fischer Scientific, Belgium. Sodium chlorite and sodium hydroxide were purchased from Bay Road, Australia. Glacial acetic acid (100 %), analytical grade, was purchased from Merck, Germany. TEMPO (2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine-1-oxyl radical; 98+%) and sodium bromide (99+%) were obtained from Thermo Fisher Scientific. All other chemicals and reagents were of analytical grade and used without any further purification.

The commercial EFB-CNF was characterized using various microscopic techniques. An optical microscope (Leica, Allendale, NJ, USA) was used to examine CNF, dried on a microscopic glass slide at different magnifications. The shape and size of CNF were studied using a field emission transmission electron microscope (FE-TEM, JEOL JEM-3100F, Tokyo, Japan) with an accelerating voltage of 300 kV. The diameter of CNF was determined using a TEM micrograph and ImageJ software. For FE-TEM analysis, the sample was prepared by adding 10 µL of 0.01 wt% homogenous suspension of CNF to a copper grid, followed by air drying. The image of the sample was then taken using FE-TEM. A tapping-mode atomic force microscope (AFM; Park Systems Model NX10, Rosemont, IL, USA) was used to investigate the surface morphology of CNF. Fiber samples were placed dropwise on a mica wafer, air-dried, and imaged with AFM. A field-emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM) (FEI Quanta 450, Hillsboro, USA) was used to examine the surface morphology of the CNF at an acceleration voltage of 5 kV. The CNF sample was placed on a double-sided tape, sputter-coated with platinum, and examined under the microscope.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Batch hydrothermal synthesis of nitrogen and sulfur doped CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The palm waste was thoroughly cleaned and washed with distilled water before being dried. The dried palm waste was then chopped, milled, and sieved to +50/−200 mesh. For the synthesis of CDs, a batch hydrothermal stainless-steel reactor with Teflon lining was used according to the synthesis temperature and time described by previous research from our group [20–22]. To synthesize N/S-CDs, palm waste (2.5 g) was added to the reactor along with 50 mL of 0.4 mol L−1 p-phenylenediamine and 0.4 mol L−1 sodium thiosulfate to produce nitrogen and sulfur doped CDs. The hydrothermal reaction was carried out at 220 °C at a pressure of 2.3 MPa for 6 h. After the reaction, the material was extracted from the solution by centrifugation at 6000 rpm for 15 min at 20 °C to separate the cellulose-rich fraction and then at 10,000 rpm to separate the CDs solution. A 0.22-micron syringe membrane filter was used to purify the N/S-CDs, which were then dialyzed using a dialysis membrane with a cutoff diameter of 1.0 kDa molecular weight.

UV–vis absorption spectroscopy was implemented to investigate the UV–vis absorption of the produced CDs in a wavelength range of 200 to 800 nm with a dual-beam spectrophotometer (Model UV 1800, Thermo Fisher Scientific, Japan). The fluorescence emission properties of N/S-CDs were measured by changing the excitation wavelength from 270 to 400 nm. A Spectrofluorometer (Model FP-6200, Perkin Elmer, Japan) was used to measure the fluorescence intensity of the CDs.

TEMPO-oxidized CNF was synthesized as per the protocol outlined by Saito et al. [23]. The reaction was carried out by adding 5 g of CNF to 100 mL of water containing 0.5 g of NaBr and 0.08 g of TEMPO. The pH of a sodium hypochlorite (12% w/v) solution was adjusted to 10 using 0.1 M HCl. The TEMPO-mediated oxidation was initiated by adding the required amount of NaClO solution (5.0 mM NaClO per gram of cellulose) and continued at 30 °C with 500 rpm stirring. The reaction pH was maintained at 10 with 0.5 M NaOH until no change in pH was observed. The TEMPO-oxidized CNF, as shown in Scheme S1, was washed multiple times with ultrapure water and stored at 4 °C for further analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Coupling of N/S-CDs to native CNF and TEMPO-CNF
ce:section.ce:para: In 50 mL of distilled water, N/S-CDs were mixed with native CNF (1% w/v) or TEMPOCNF (1% w/v). These samples were placed in a Teflon-lined autoclave and kept in an oven for 4 h at 120 °C. The names CD@CNF and T-CD@CNF were assigned for N/S doped CDs coupled with native CNF and N/S doped CDs coupled with TEMPOCNF, respectively. The same reaction mixture for coupling N/S doped CDs to native CNF and TEMPO-modified CNF was carried out at 30 °C at 500 rpm for 4 h and named CD-CNF and T-CD-CNF, respectively. After cooling the reaction at room temperature, samples were centrifuged at 6000 rpm to separate unbound N/S-CDs. The reaction was washed multiple times before being freeze-dried and finally stored in the refrigerator (∼4 °C).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of pristine, surface modified and CD bound CNF
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the change in functional groups, Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy was utilized. The FT-IR spectra (Nicolet 6700, Thermo Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA) were acquired in the 500–4000 cm−1 range, with 64 scans at a resolution of 4 cm−1. The measurements were conducted in the attenuated total reflectance (ATR) mode. The chemical composition of the CDs, pristine, TEMPO-oxidized, and CDs coupled CNF, was quantitatively analyzed using X-ray fluorescence (XRF) (Horiba Scientific, France). X-ray diffraction spectrometer (Bruker D8-Discover, Germany) was used to determine the crystalline properties of pristine, TEMPO-oxidized, and CDs coupled CNF. The XRD analysis was carried out using a XRD spectrometer equipped with a Cu tube at 2θ of 20–80° in 0.02° steps at a scan rate of 1° per step.

The crystallinity index (CrI) was calculated from the difference in XRD intensity between I200 (crystalline structure at 22.5°) and IAm (amorphous structure at 18°) divided by I200 as described in a previous study [24] as shown in Eq. (1).

The charges on CNF were measured in triplicate using a Zeta-sizer (Malvern Nano ZS, Malvern, Herrenberg, Germany) at 25 °C. The applied electrode voltage was 3.4 V in a 16 mm spacing cell. The ζ-potential was recorded at pH 7.0, which was set using 0.1 mol L−1 NaOH and HCl. To increase fiber dispersion, CNF (0.01% w/v) was ultrasonicated and vortexed for 2 min before the analysis. Three individual measurements were used to calculate the standard deviation and mean values of zeta potentials. A thermogravimetric analyzer (NETZSCH, STA 449 F3 Jupiter, Selb, Germany) was used to examine the thermal properties of CNF. Under nitrogen gas purge, CNF samples (15 mg) were heated from 30 °C to 800 °C at a heating rate of 10 °C min−1 until no weight change was noticed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell culture and in-vitro toxicity study of hybrid cellulose membranes
ce:section.ce:para: Mouse fibroblast cells (L-929), obtained from the American Type Culture Collection (ATCC) (Manassas, Virginia, USA) were cultured in Dulbecco's modified eagle medium (DMEM) containing 5 % v/v fetal bovine serum [25]. Cells were grown at 37 °C in 5 % CO2 humidified air and passaged twice a week. When the cells reached 85 % confluence, they were harvested using 0.25 % trypsin and sub-cultured.

The MTT (3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)−2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide) assay was used to assess cell viability, following the protocol described by Mossman with some modifications [26]. L-929 cells (1 × 105 cells mL−1) were seeded into a 96-well plate, a volume of 100 μL per well, and incubated at 37 °C in 5 % CO2 for 24 h. The DMEM medium was then removed, and 100 μL of CNF samples (1000 µg mL−1) was added and incubated at 37 °C in 5 % CO2 for 24 h. After 24 h of treatment, the culture medium was replaced with 100 μL of fresh medium containing MTT (5 mg mL−1) and further incubated for 4 h at the same experimental condition. The MTT assay is based on the formation of colored formazan by the mitochondrial dehydrogenase enzyme in metabolically active cells. The resulting formazan was then dissolved in a solution of 100 % DMSO and 10 % Sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS). Plates containing cell culture were centrifuged, and the supernatant was transferred to a fresh microtiter plate and read at 570 nm using a microplate reader (BIORAD-680, USA). The percent cell viability was calculated as mentioned by Khan et al. [25] using Eq. (2), where A and B are the absorbances of the control cells solution and treated cells solution, respectively.
(2)

A vacuum filtration system was used to fabricate functional hybrid cellulose membranes. Prior to vacuum filtration, 10 wt% CNF slurry was diluted to 0.25% w/v by adding ultrapure water. Under vacuum (36 kPa), 100 mL of 0.25% w/v pristine CNF or modified CNF was mixed with 0.5% w/v commercial cellulose fibers and filtered. After filtration, samples were carefully transferred to Teflon plates and pressed between two plates with 5 kg weight. Blotting paper was placed on top of the membranes to expedite drying, and the whole setup was placed in an oven at 40 °C for 48 h. The blotting paper was changed every 10 h. The thickness of the membranes was measured using a micrometer, which varied by different volumes and concentrations of CNF and commercial cellulose fibers in the solution.

The N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm and the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) analyzer (Micromeritics ASAP 2000, Stockholm, Sweden) were used to evaluate the surface area, size, and distribution of pores in the hybrid membranes. Prior to analysis, the samples were degassed in a dry N2 flow for 24 h at 80 °C. The surface characteristics of the pristine and modified cellulose membranes were observed with a FE-SEM (FEI Quanta 450, Hillsboro, USA). The elemental composition (particularly sulfur, which displays coupling of N/S-CDs to CNF) in the membrane was measured both qualitatively and quantitively by SEM-EDX. The mechanical properties of the membranes were measured using a universal mechanical testing machine (INSTRON, 5980 Dual Column Floor Frames, USA). The measurement was performed using a gauge length of 20 mm with a test specimen of 15 mm in nominal width. A preload of 1 N and a strain rate of 2 mm min−1 were used. The stress was defined as σ = F/A0, where F is the force at break (N) and A0 is the area of the cross-section of the tensile sample (m2). The strain was calculated as ε = ln((L-L0)/L0), in which L0 is the initial sample length (m) and L is the sample length at break (m).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Air filtration of hybrid cellulose membranes
ce:section.ce:para:
The non-viable particle filtration efficiency (PFE) of the test article was measured using the PFE standard test method described in ASTM F2299–03 (CPC, TSI Incorporated Model 3788, St. Paul, Minnesota, USA). Monodispersed polystyrene latex (PSL) beads were nebulized and passed through the test article. Detailed information on the test method can be found in the Supporting information and Table S1. The PFE of the membrane frameworks for a known particle size was calculated using the following Eq. (3), which was given by Nallathambi et al. [27], where η1 is the number of inlet particles and η2 is the number of outlet particles.

A microscopic analysis was conducted to investigate the properties of commercial CNF, produced from palm bunch. The result demonstrated that the nanofibers in the medium were of uniform size and distribution (Fig. 1a ). The FE-TEM study showed that the length of CNF was significantly large (Fig. 1b), while the average diameter of CNFs was ∼23.18 nm (Fig. 1b, inset). The FE-SEM analysis revealed that the surface of CNF appears smooth and interfibrous (Fig. 1c). At the same time, AFM image provides nanoscale information about the smooth surface and consistent nanofibrils of CNF in Fig. 1d. AFM analysis was unable to accurately determine the length and diameter of nanofibers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of nitrogen and sulfur doped CDs
ce:section.ce:para: CDs were successfully synthesized from palm waste through a hydrothermal reaction at 220 °C for 6 h. Fig. 2a (inset) shows optical images of undoped CDs and N/S-CDs under UV irradiation. In an aqueous medium under UV irradiation at 365 nm, undoped CDs displayed light brown-green fluorescence, whereas N/S-CDs displayed strong green fluorescence (Fig. 2a, inset). It has been reported that N- and S- doped CDs resulted in red-shift phenomena to longer wavelength emission. Thus, blue emissive CDs was altered to green emissive N/S-CDs [20].

Depending on the amount of nitrogen (from p-phenylenediamine) and sulfur (from sodium thiosulfate) used in N/S-CDs synthesis, the composition of the CDs cores transformed from amorphous to graphitic structure. Fig. 2a depicts the UV–vis absorption spectroscopy of undoped CDs and N/S-CDs. The result demonstrates an absorption peak at 265 nm for undoped CDs, which was apparently assigned to the π→π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains of conjugated CC bonds concomitant with a carbon core [28]. The N/S-CDs showed maximum absorbance at 400 nm, which is due to transitions from CO bonds or NH2 and SO3H functional groups [29,30]. The literature study documented that the absorption peaks at longer wavelengths (350–400 nm) might be due to the trapping of excited state energy from high-density nitrogen and sulfur at the passivated surface [31].

Fig. 2b shows the fluorescence characteristics of N/S-CDs with different excitation wavelengths of λEx = 270–400 nm. The maximum emission wavelength was demonstrated at λEm = 422 nm when excited at λEx = 400 nm. It was observed that the doping of nitrogen and/or sulfur atoms remarkably increased the fluorescence intensity of CDs (Fig. 2b) [32]. The maximum quantum yield (QY) of 68.7 % was recorded for N/S-CDs. It has been reported that the heteroatoms create defects in CDs that trap the excitation energy and thus increase the photoluminescence (PL) impact of CDs. In CDs, sulfur atoms play a crucial role as catalysts for an oxidation–reduction reaction; hence, the presence of a sulfur heteroatom presumably introduces more surface defects on CDs and further enhances photoluminescence [33,34].

N and S doping onto CD structures can cause a reduction of energy bandgap or photonic bandgap [20,35]. Narrowing energy bandgap of nanomaterials, which is in this case N/S-CDs, can facilitate more effective electron mobility from its ground state to its excited state, particularly under visible light or UV light excitation. After doping with N and S, electrons require less energy to jump between the gap, and thus, electrons will effortlessly transport to reactant atoms to catalyze the reaction. Moreover, the electron transfer from the ground state to the excited state can cause an electron hole to occur. In the realms of physics, chemistry, and electronic engineering, an electron hole (h +), often referred to simply as a "hole," represents a quasiparticle indicating the absence of an electron in a position within an atom or atomic lattice. In a standard atom or crystal lattice, the equilibrium between the negative charge of electrons and the positive charge of atomic nuclei is disrupted when an electron is missing, resulting in a net positive charge at the location of the hole. In the context of redox reactions, holes generated through light absorption can interact with water molecules (or hydroxide anions), leading to the formation of hydroxyl radicals: H2O + h + → HO· + H +. Alternatively, these holes can be filled by an adsorbed organic donor. Debates persist in the scientific literature regarding the oxidative species involved. Although photogenerated holes possess the potential to oxidize various substrates through electron transfer, the prevailing pathway in aqueous solutions appears to be the oxidation of water to hydroxyl radicals. Additionally, the superoxide anion can serve as an oxidant in this process. The overall result of this sequence can be the complete mineralization of organic compounds [36].

The FT-IR spectra of the unaltered, TEMPO-modified, and CD-coupled CNF were studied to find out how the CDs bind to the CNF. The results are shown in Fig. 3a . The FT-IR spectrum of CNF showed the cellulose fingerprint region at 1400–850 cm−1 and carboxylate group peak at 1606 cm−1 in TEMPO-oxidized CNF as the hydroxyl group of CNF was converted into a carboxylate group via TEMPO-oxidation [37,38]. Similarly, both the T-CD-CNF and T-CD@CNF had the cellulose fingerprint region (1400–850 cm−1), while the peaks of the glucuronate group (peaks at 1606 cm−1) were altered by the addition of the CDs. The peaks of the carboxylate group were reduced, indicating a shift in the carboxylate group's ionic state. Furthermore, peaks at 1719 cm−1 arose in the CD-coupled CNF, which could be attributed to CD functional groups, i.e., amide and carboxyl groups. There was no significant change in the fingerprint region of CNF, while the peak maxima at 1719 cm−1 and 1604 cm−1 attributed to the amide group and carboxyl group, respectively, were increased [39].

The XRD patterns of the pristine CNF, TEMPOCNF, and CD-coupled CNF are shown in Fig. 3b; and the crystallinity percentages are demonstrated in Table 1 . The XRD patterns of pristine CNF revealed a cellulose-I crystal structure that was not altered even after in-situ hydrothermal coupling of CDs to CNF [40]. The CD's typical peaks were not observed at any of the examined locations, possibly because of the very small quantity and homogenous distribution. The overall number of CDs grown on the CNF was insufficient to affect the XRD pattern. The crystallinity of the CNF was calculated using Eq. (1). It was found that the crystallinity of the pristine CNF was 55.84 %, and it was almost similar (55.83 %) after TEMPO-oxidation. The crystallinity of the CD-CNF and T-CD-CNF was 54.92 % and 51.67 %, respectively, and these values were lower than the pristine CNF (Table 1). The crystallinity of CNF arose slightly during in-situ binding under hydrothermal conditions, reaching 57.48 % for CD@CNF and 57.10 % for T-CD@CNF. These data showed that there was no significant change in the crystalline properties of CNF. The TEMPO oxidization causes hydroxyl group conversion to carboxylate in cellulose. Mild hydrothermal carbonization can degrade the carboxylate group in cellulose, which is less stable than the glucose. The acidic condition regarding the hot compressed water state may slightly decompose and hydrolyze the CNF during the in-situ procedure. As a result, CD@CNF and T-CD@CNF exhibit slightly higher crystallinity than pristine CNF (Table 1). Even after hydrothermal treatment, the cellulose crystal structure remained the same as observed by unchanged XRD patterns [41,42].

The surface zeta potential of pristine, TEMPO modified, and CD coupled CNF was studied in pH 7.0 and is presented in Fig. 3c and Table 1. The zeta potential of CNF suspension was −38.95 mV, which increased significantly after TEMPO oxidation to −54.7 mV (Table 1). After coupling with CDs, the zeta potential of the CNF suspension decreased slightly. This indicates that a significant amount of the carboxylate groups was either removed or blocked by the CDs. After binding with CDs, the values of zeta potential were lower for TEMPO-modified than unmodified CNF, which might be because carboxylate groups were blocked more prominently in TEMPO-modified CNF.

Thermogravimetric analysis was used to assess the thermal stability of the pristine, TEMPO-modified, and CD-coupled CNF (Fig. 3d & 3e; Table 1). The decomposition of pristine CNF began at 290 °C, whereas the TEMPO-modified CNF showed a lower decomposition temperature (275 °C). The thermal decomposition behaviors of CNF were altered by the binding of CD, irrespective of the binding mechanism employed, whether at room temperature or at 120 °C under hydrothermal conditions. The first decomposition temperatures of all CNF samples were identical, and these were ranged from 290 °C to 310 °C, except for TEMPO-oxidized CNF, which showed a commenced decomposition at 275 °C. A comparison of the decomposition temperatures showed that the CD-bound CNF decomposed at a higher temperature. Thus, the interaction between CDs and CNF improved CNF's thermal stability. The CDs used secondary and main bonding in CD-CNF and CD@CNF to interface with the CNF. In the case of CD-CNF, the surface of the CD was covered by the CNF, preventing the degradation of functional groups. In contrast, the rise in degradation temperature of CD@CNF can be attributed to the novel chemical bonding, which may improve material thermal stability [43]. The thermal stability behaviors of the CNF were best with T-CD-CNF and T-CD@CNF, as both samples had the highest starting decomposition temperature and the maximum rate of decomposition among all samples. In summary, CDs improved the CNF's thermal stability. According to the literature, thermal stability is mostly influenced by CD content [43].

Table S2 shows the XRF profile of CDs, pristine CNF, TEMPO-modified CNF, and CDs coupled CNF. Table S2 shows that the sulfur content was 90.52, 14.71, 23.43, 5.28, and 12.90 w% in CDs, CD-CNF, CD@CNF, T-CD-CNF, and T-CD@CNF, respectively. The sulfur was not detected in pristine and TEMPO-oxidized CNF. The data showed that the CDs were significantly bound to both CD@CNF and T-CD@CNF.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In-vitro toxicity study of pristine and modified CNF
ce:section.ce:para: The viability of mouse fibroblast cells, treated with pristine and modified CNF at 1000 µg mL−1 concentration for 24 h of treatment, was evaluated by MTT assay, as shown in Fig. 3f. The microscopic images of the control and treated L929 cells were taken with an inverted microscope (Nikon ECLIPSE TS100, MC4KW-G1) at 100 × magnification and are shown in Fig. S1. The viability of L929 cells was 68.87 % with a 1000 μg mL−1 pristine CNF concentration. Maximum cell viability was reported with CD-CNF, and it was 98.72 %. TEMPO-modified CNF displayed a cell viability of 90.70 %. The cell viability was higher with CD-coupled CNF, which showed that the CDs reduced the cytotoxic properties of CNF. The reason was possibly because CDs exhibit potential anti-inflammatory therapeutic effects owing to their high efficiency in promoting electron transfer for scavenging reactive radical species (ROS) in the biological system [44]. The results showed that exposure to CD-CNF has no significant toxicity to mouse fibroblast cells. Previous studies revealed that cotton CNF has no toxic effect at lower concentrations (0–100 µg mL−1), while at high CNF concentrations (5000 µg mL−1), the cell viability decreases up to 36.51 % [45]. Lopes et al. studied the cytotoxicity, oxidative stress, and cytokine secretion of different CNF in human skin, lung, and immune cells [46]. There was no induction of oxidative stress in treated cells, but a slight elevation in pro-inflammatory cytokine levels was reported. The study demonstrates the utility of CNF in biomedical products and implants where cell viability is critical.

Fig. 4 shows the surface and cross-section of the EFB-CNF-M and CD@CNF-M framework, displaying both the support and functional fibers. In the high magnification of membrane images, both the functional and support fibers were clearly visible, while the coupling of CDs to the CNF was not seen clearly as the size of the CDs was <10 nm. In vacuum filtration, a 2D paper-like structure was formed, showing a compact nanofiber network. The cross-sectional analysis of the membrane structure confirmed a single-layered structure, which clearly indicates a mixed network between CNF and cellulose fibers.

The elemental compositions (C, O, and S) in EFB-CNF-M, TEMPOCNF-M, CD@CNF-M, and T-CD@CNF-M were analyzed using SEM-EDX technique, and the data are shown in Fig. 5 . The sulfur was not detected in EFB-CNF-M, TEMPOCNF-M. In contrast, 0.1 wt% and 0.2 wt% of sulfur were observed in CD@CNF-M (c) and T-CD@CNF-M (d), respectively. CDs coupled membrane exhibited a very small amount of sulfur as it were doped to CDs that was present in the cellulose membranes. The SEM-EDX images of the mapping of elements in the membranes demonstrated an even and homogeneous distribution of CDs in the membranes (Fig. S2).

A tensile strength test was used to study the mechanical properties of membranes made from pristine, TEMPO-modified, and CD-coupled CNF, as illustrated in Fig. 7 and Table S3. The tensile strength of the pristine CNF membrane (EFB-CNF-M) was 6.3 ± 1.6 MPa (4.0 ± 0.9 % elongation at break), which was relatively low among all membranes. Tarrés and colleagues reported a significant enhancement in paper strength with the incorporation of CNF that can be attributed to the extensive surface area offered by CNF [50]. The presence of carboxylate and hydroxyl groups within the dense and layered CNF fibers fosters the formation of additional hydrogen bonds between the nanofibers, consequently bolstering the tensile strength [51]. The coupling of CDs to CNF at 30 °C significantly increased the tensile strength of the membrane. The results showed that T-CD-CNF-M had the highest tensile strength, 9.3 ± 1.9 MPa (3.0 ± 0.5 % elongation at break) among all the membranes evaluated, followed by CD-CNF-M (9.0 ± 1.3 MPa and 3.2 ± 0.5 % elongation at break). The tensile strength of the TEMPO-modified CNF membrane was obviously higher, owing to an increase in the number of interconnections between nanofibers from the modification. The carboxylate groups in the TEMPOCNF were reported to influence an increased tensile strength [52]. However, the reaction for coupling of CD with CNF and TEMPOCNF at higher temperatures (120 °C) led to a slight reduction of the mechanical strength of the membranes (6.8 ± 1.5 MPa for CD@CNF-M and 5.2 ± 1.2 MPa for T-CD@CNF-M). Therefore, CDs coupling onto hydroxy and carboxylate groups of pristine CNF and TEMPOCNF, respectively, is more favorable at room temperature. The tensile test results showed that the TEMPO modified CNF membrane was stronger with moderate flexibility and stiffness, which agrees well with previous reports [53,54]. The significant interactions and compatibility between the CD and CNF were attributed to the superior mechanical properties of the CD-bound CNF membranes. The tensile strength of CDs coupling with TEMPO modified CNF membranes at room temperature (T-CD-CNF-M) from the present work demonstrated that the membrane fabricated from TEMPO oxidized CNF and coupling with CDs at room temperature can be used for air filtration and the removal of PM and aerosol volatile organic compounds (VOCs) [14].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of pristine and modified cellulose membranes for air filtration
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 8 depicts the PFE of the functionalized membrane framework. At 0.1 µm particle size, the PFE values ranged from 48.94 % and 67.45 % for T-CD-CNF-M and EFB-CNF-M, respectively. Nevertheless, for CD@CNF-M and TEMPO-CNF-M membranes, the PFE values were 52.62 % and 52.39 %, respectively. The PFE of the EFB-CNF membrane was 67.47 %, showing lower PFE with 0.1 µm particle size, which was increased to 80.85 % when 0.3 µm particle size was used in filtration. The increment in PEF with 0.3 µm particles was demonstrated not only in EFB-CNF-M but also in all the membranes tested. The PEF of other membranes were 52.39 %, 58.99 %, 52.62 %, 48.94 %, and 59.61 % for TEMPOCNF-M, CD-CNF-M, CD@CNF-M, T-CD-CNF-M, T-CD@CNF-M, respectively, which was increased significantly when the particle size was 0.3 µm. The maximum PFE for EFB-CNF-M tested for 0.3 µm particle filtration was 80.85 %, while for TEMPO-CNF-M, CD-CNF-M, and T-CD@CNF-M were 71.72 %, 70.04 %, and 71.76 %, respectively (Table S3).

The diminished PFE of these membranes is presumably due to a lower concentration of CNF in comparison to the concentration of cellulose microfiber utilized as a supporting fiber in membrane construction. The functional CNF concentration in all the membranes was 0.25 wt%, while the microfiber concentration was 0.50 wt%. PFE can be raised by increasing the functional CNF content. In the case of differential pressure across the membrane (ΔP), pristine CNF exhibited the lowest ΔP. In contrast, the TEMPO-modified CNF and CD coupling membranes demonstrated higher ΔP because of the smaller pore size of TEMPO-modified CNF that was coupling with CDs when compared with pristine CNF membrane, as demonstrated in Table 2. It has been documented that the formation of a surface porosity that can sustain the equilibrium between filtration capacity and pressure drop with a single ultrathin layer of filter material has been facilitated by the micro-dimension of the fibers [55]. In addition, the removal efficiency of a filter membrane is known to be dependent on its surface area. Therefore, an increase in surface area led to a rise in overall removal efficiency [56]. Besides the porosity of membranes, there are several parameters, including particle dispersion, aerosol charge, and face velocity, that have been shown to influence the filtration performance of fibrous filter materials [57].

Another aspect that influences filtration efficiency is particle charge. When compared to charged aerosols, neutralized aerosols have higher penetration (poor filtration efficiency). Cellulose filter membranes include a lot of OH groups, which could cause interactions between cellulose fibers and particles [58]. Apart from the charge, the particle size distribution has a significant impact on the determination of filtration efficiency. The filtration efficiency varies depending on the size of the aerosol [59]. Smaller particles are collected via diffusion, while bigger particles are captured via interception and impaction, depending on their aerodynamic dimensions [60]. It has been shown that implying structural filter heterogeneity has also influenced filter performance and resistance and that one should seek to minimize this effect by making nanofibrous filters as homogenous as possible [61].

The importance of functional groups, mechanical properties, and filtration efficiency of the fabricated nanocellulose membrane frameworks are presented in this study. A single-step hydrothermal reaction worked well to couple CDs to CNF without much changing the fibrous structure of CNF. The CD-coupled CNF membranes remarkably enhanced the tensile strength of the membranes. The bonding between CDs (nitrogen and sulfur groups of N/S-CDs) and CNF makes the CD-coupled CNF membrane have superior characteristics in terms of mechanical tensile strength, thermal stability, and nanoporosity. We can conclude that the synthesis of fibrous filters incorporating nanofibers and the fabrication of membranes utilizing the vacuum filtration method are promising techniques for improving the filtration of aerosol particles. The pristine CNF membrane exhibits PEF (80.85 %) for neutral aerosol particles of 0.3 μm at a low-pressure difference across the membrane, which could be increased further by increasing the concentration of CNF in the membranes. In addition, the surface functional groups and ζ-potential of the CNF gave a picture of the possible filtration application for charged particles, especially microbes, since the cell walls of the microorganisms contain charged amino acids. Thus, the surface-functionalized membranes might be more effective than the non-functionalized membranes for microbial retention. The contributions of this study can lead to new lines in the commercialization of cost-effective, biocompatible, and biodegradable nanocellulose filters and nano-porous membranes for air filtration.
